chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Methods of Separation in Everyday Life

points: Handpicking is used for separating solid materials (stones from wheat/rice) based on difference in size, colour, and shape.
Threshing involves beating harvested stalks to separate grains.
Winnowing separates heavier grains from lighter husk using wind or blowing air (Activity 9.1).
Sieving is employed to separate solid mixtures of different particle sizes (bran from flour, pebbles from sand).
Sedimentation is the settling of heavier insoluble components at the bottom of a liquid.
Decantation involves removing the clear liquid by tilting the vessel after sedimentation.
Filtration uses a filter (cloth, paper, sand) to remove fine insoluble parts (Activity 9.4).
Evaporation converts liquid to vapour to recover dissolved solids like salt from salt solution (Activity 9.3).
Churning is used to extract butter from curd (centrifugation principle).
Magnetic Separation uses magnets to pull magnetic substances like iron nails from sawdust or general waste (Fig. 9.12).
Historical Context: Salt evaporation connects to the Dandi March; Ayurveda uses evaporation to dry medicinal plant parts.
Environmental Note: Rivers and oceans face plastic pollution, which can be trapped in fishing nets along with fish.
